title: Why Different Gemstones Have Different Mineral Recipes

lesson.learn:
Gemstones differ because they are built from different minerals, crystal structures, and trace impurities. Ruby and sapphire are both corundum; amethyst is quartz; emerald is beryl. Tiny changes in chemistry can completely change color, hardness, and value. That's why two stones can look similar to a casual eye but behave very differently under a microscope.

Gemstones are therefore geology made beautiful. Their chemistry is what gives them color, clarity, and character.
lesson.simpler: Gemstones look different because they are made of different minerals and tiny chemical differences change their color and structure.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Gemstone variety comes from mineral chemistry, not just appearance.
lesson.deeperDive:
The same mineral family can produce many gemstones. Corundum becomes ruby when chromium gives it a red color, or sapphire when other trace elements shift the hue. Quartz can appear as amethyst, citrine, or smoky quartz depending on impurities and radiation history. That is what makes gemology such a rich mix of mineralogy, chemistry, and optics.

Cutting and clarity matter too, because a gemstone's internal structure influences how light travels through it. The stone may come from geology, but the sparkle is a dialogue between crystal and light.
lesson.funFact: A tiny impurity can change a gemstone's color completely.
references: Gemological Institute of America - gemstone basics
Mindat - mineral species and gem varieties

people: Jewelers - people who cut and set stones
Collectors - people who value rare specimens

years: Present day - gemstones are studied with scientific precision
concepts: Mineral species - a specific chemical and crystal structure
Corundum - the mineral behind ruby and sapphire
Beryl - the mineral family that includes emerald
Impurity - a trace element that changes color
